planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring or fall in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; autumn cherry blooms in October–November and again in spring, and the cultivar 'Autumnalis' is widely planted for its remarkable ability to produce flowers in warm fall weather as well as the main spring flush.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; autumn cherry is a Japanese species celebrated as the parent of the cultivar 'Autumnalis', which holds the exceptional distinction of producing semi-double, pale pink flowers twice — in October-November when temperatures drop in fall, and again in early spring. In mild winters, the fall flowering can continue sporadically through winter into spring, providing flowers for weeks or months. The weeping forms (var. pendula) create graceful, cascading silhouettes, and 'Autumnalis Rosea' has deeper pink, semi-double flowers. More cold-hardy than many ornamental cherries and longer-lived than double-flowering varieties. A cherished ornamental for fall-winter garden interest, providing color at a time when almost no other flowering trees are in bloom. Less vigorous than Yoshino cherry but more refined.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees or shrubs in spring or fall in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Fuji cherry is among the earliest ornamental cherries to bloom, often flowering in February. Its compact size and early bloom date make it suitable for smaller gardens where large cherries would be too imposing.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Fuji cherry is a Japanese alpine species from the slopes of Mount Fuji, characteristically small and shrubby compared to the large ornamental cherries, bearing small, finely cut, incised-margined leaves (incisa = cut) and a profusion of tiny white to pale pink flowers from February to March — among the earliest cherries to bloom. The small stature makes it appropriate for small garden spaces and courtyard gardens where full-sized cherries would be overwhelming. Good fall color in shades of orange and red provides a second season of interest. Important parent in breeding compact ornamental cherry cultivars, including the popular columnar 'Amanogawa' cherry. The widely planted Okame cherry (Prunus x incam 'Okame') is a cross between P. incisa and P. campanulata (Taiwan cherry).

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring or fall in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; flowering almond blooms on old wood — prune immediately after flowering to promote vigorous new growth that will carry next year's flowers. Do not prune in fall or winter, which removes the flower buds.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; flowering almond is a Chinese shrub long cultivated in Asian gardens, producing small, double-flowered blooms so densely that stems are buried in flowers from tip to base in early spring. The double pink cultivar 'Sinensis' and double white 'Alba Plena' are the most widely grown forms. Unlike most Prunus shrubs, flowering almond has little wildlife value (the double flowers limit pollinator access and fruit rarely sets) but provides exceptional ornamental impact in the spring garden. After flowering, the plant is an unremarkable medium deciduous shrub, so site it where it can be seen from a window during its brief but spectacular early spring bloom and is less conspicuous the rest of the year. Prune hard immediately after bloom to maintain a compact, floriferous shape.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring or fall in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; purple-leaf plum blooms very early in spring — often in February — before the leaves emerge. The species is the parent of numerous purple-foliage cultivars widely used in landscaping.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; purple-leaf plum (cherry plum) is a small Asian ornamental tree and one of the most widely planted landscape trees in North America for its combination of very early white to pale pink spring flowers — often blooming in February in zones 7–9 — and the dramatic burgundy-purple foliage retained through the summer. The species itself has green leaves, but the purple-leaved cultivars 'Atropurpurea', 'Thundercloud', 'Krauter Vesuvius', and 'Newport' are among the most common ornamental trees in American residential landscaping. Small, round, yellow to red plums are produced in summer and are edible, making acceptable jam and jelly. Self-sows readily and some cultivars can become invasive outside gardens in mild climates. Short-lived compared to other ornamental trees (15–25 years) but very fast-growing and reliably floriferous.

planting.note: Plant hardwood cuttings in early spring in moist to wet, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; narrowleaf cottonwood grows naturally along mountain streams and rivers of the Rocky Mountain region and is the primary cottonwood of riparian habitats in the high-elevation west. Best propagated by cuttings as seed viability is very short.

directions: Plant hardwood cuttings in early spring in moist to wet, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral riparian soil in full sun; narrowleaf cottonwood is the dominant riparian tree of mountain streams and rivers in the Rocky Mountain west, forming gallery forest along watercourses from Montana to New Mexico. The distinctively narrow, willow-like leaves set it apart from other cottonwoods, giving it a more refined, graceful appearance. In fall, the foliage turns brilliant golden-yellow — a signature of Rocky Mountain fall scenery, particularly in high-elevation valleys where large stands create sweeping landscapes of fall gold against pine forest. The tree provides critical nesting habitat for raptors, heron colonies, and songbirds in mountain riparian corridors. Hybridizes freely with plains cottonwood where ranges overlap, producing intermediate forms. A fast-growing pioneer species of mountain stream banks and flood plains in the intermountain west.
